mapQuestions.question: What sea surrounds most of Italy?
What is the capital of Italy?
mapQuestions.answer: Rome. The whole of Vatican City fits inside Rome.

mapQuestions.question: What shape is Italy?
mapQuestions.answer: A boot, kicking a small triangular island (Sicily) into the sea.

mapQuestions.question: What mountain ranges run through Italy?
mapQuestions.answer: The Alps in the north, the Apennines down the spine.

foods.name: Pizza Margherita
foods.description: Tomato, mozzarella, basil — the colours of the Italian flag. Real pizza here is wood-fired and thin.

foods.name: Fresh pasta
foods.description: Italy has over 300 pasta shapes. Try carbonara, cacio e pepe, or whatever the regional speciality is.

foods.name: Gelato
foods.description: Smoother and less fatty than ice cream. Pick a local fruit flavour like pistachio, lemon, or strawberry.

foods.name: Focaccia
foods.description: Soft flatbread baked with olive oil and salt. Great for breakfast or with a meal.

foods.name: Caprese salad
foods.description: Sliced tomato, fresh mozzarella, basil leaves. Three ingredients, one of the best things Italians eat.

foods.name: Espresso
foods.description: Italians drink it standing at the bar for half the price. Don't order a cappuccino after lunch — it's a breakfast drink here.

scavengerItems.label: A Vespa scooter parked on a cobbled street
An old church with a bell tower (campanile)
Hand-rolled pasta drying in a shop window
A street cat lounging on warm stone
Someone playing music in a piazza
Ancient ruins poking out of the modern city
A marble fountain people are tossing coins into
Hand gestures in a passionate conversation
A Latin inscription on an old building

geoMatches.description: The capital. Colosseum, Trevi Fountain, Pantheon — and Vatican City sits inside it like a country within a country.
A city built on 118 islands. No cars, just canals, bridges, and gondolas. Slowly sinking by a few millimetres a year.
Rolling hills, vineyards, cypress trees, Florence and Pisa. The countryside of every postcard you've ever seen.
The football the Italian boot is kicking. Mount Etna (still active!), Greek ruins, and incredible food.
Five colourful villages clinging to cliffs above the sea. Connected only by hiking paths and trains.

senses.smell: Fresh basil, espresso, sun-warmed stone, oregano, salt sea air…
senses.hear: Church bells, Vespa scooters, the hiss of espresso machines, "Ciao!" everywhere…
senses.taste: Tomato and mozzarella, salt and olive oil, cold gelato, strong espresso, fresh bread…
senses.feel: Cobblestones under your shoes, warm stone in piazzas, ice-cold gelato on a hot day…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is the Colosseum still standing after 2000 years?
stories.body: The Colosseum was built around 80 AD as a giant Roman sports arena. It held up to 80,000 people watching gladiator fights, mock sea battles (they actually flooded it!), and wild animal hunts. The Romans invented concrete using volcanic ash from Mount Vesuvius — that concrete is still holding up the building today. Earthquakes and stone-thieves have taken bits, but most of it is still there. You're walking on the same stones Romans walked on 2000 years ago.

stories.question: Why are there no cars in Venice?
stories.body: Venice was built on 118 small islands in a marshy lagoon over 1500 years ago. People wanted to hide from invaders, and the lagoon protected them. They drove wooden poles deep into the mud to build on, and used canals as streets. Today there are still no cars — only boats. Water taxis, water buses, ambulance boats, even post-office boats. The wooden poles holding the city up are still mostly the originals.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your home town had canals instead of roads, what would change about your daily life?

stories.question: What happened to the town buried by a volcano?
stories.body: In 79 AD the volcano Mount Vesuvius erupted suddenly and buried the Roman town of Pompeii under five metres of ash and rock. The eruption was so fast that everything was preserved in place — people, dogs, food on tables, graffiti on walls. Archaeologists rediscovered the town in 1748, almost perfectly frozen for nearly 1700 years. You can walk down Roman streets and see bakeries with bread still in the ovens. Vesuvius is still active — Naples is right next to it.

stories.question: Why is Florence called the birthplace of the Renaissance?
stories.body: The Renaissance (about 1400–1600) was a period when European art, science, and ideas exploded. Florence was the centre of it. Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Botticelli, and Galileo all worked there. The wealthy Medici family paid artists to make stunning paintings, sculptures, and buildings. Michelangelo's 5-metre marble statue of David was carved from a single block of stone. The Renaissance changed how Europeans thought — they started believing in science, art, and individual humans, not just kings and the church.
stories.thinkingQuestion: "Renaissance" means "rebirth". What's something you'd like to rediscover or do better the second time round?

stories.question: Why did Italy only become one country in 1861?
stories.body: For most of history "Italy" wasn't a country — it was a patchwork of small kingdoms, papal states, and city republics like Venice, Florence, and Naples. Each had its own laws, currency, and even language (the dialects still differ today). In the 1800s a movement called the Risorgimento ("rising again") united them. The hero of unification was Giuseppe Garibaldi, who led volunteer soldiers in red shirts to conquer Sicily and southern Italy. By 1861 it was finally one nation. That's why every Italian region still feels so distinct — they used to be separate countries.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Italy so far?
Italians say "la dolce vita" — the sweet life. What would your sweet life look like?
Would you rather live in Rome, Venice, or a hill town in Tuscany?
If we could time-travel to one Italian moment, which would you pick — ancient Rome, Renaissance Florence, or Venice in its glory days?
What's one Italian habit you'd like to bring home with you?
Italy has 60 UNESCO World Heritage Sites — more than any other country. Why do you think that is?

wordSearch.hint: Italian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Street cat
animals.description: Italy has millions of street cats — protected by law. Look in Rome's ruins (especially Largo di Torre Argentina, the official cat sanctuary).

animals.name: Swallows over piazzas
animals.description: In summer they dart and shriek over open squares at dusk, catching insects.

animals.name: Mallard duck
animals.description: On every fountain, canal, and park pond — Venice canals are full of them.

animals.name: Wild boar (cinghiale)
animals.description: In Tuscan forests, Roman parks, and increasingly on city outskirts. Don't approach.

animals.name: Carriage horse
animals.description: Pulling tourists around Rome and Florence — declining as cities pass animal welfare rules.

animals.name: Marsican brown bear
animals.description: About 50 left in Abruzzo National Park. Critically endangered.

animals.name: Italian wolf
animals.description: Returning to the Apennines after centuries of persecution. Heard more than seen.
